critic_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT:

The university retention model evaluation has a fundamental methodological problem: the model's predictions drove intervention assignment, and the intervention changed the outcome used to evaluate the model's predictive accuracy.

Specifically: students placed in the new model's top-risk quintile received weekly tutoring check-ins and emergency microgrants. These directly improve spring re-enrollment probability. If a high-risk student re-enrolls because of microgrant support, the model appears to have correctly identified a non-returner — but the student was not a non-returner in this scenario because the model's prediction triggered intervention that changed the trajectory. The evaluation is post-treatment and circular.

The cross-year comparison compounds this: the old heuristic was evaluated on last year's cohort (without the structured support program), while the new model was evaluated on this year's cohort (with the structured support program available). These are not equivalent evaluation conditions. Additionally, pandemic-era attendance rules were in effect last year but not this year, and cohort characteristics may differ.

The paper concludes the score should guide retention investment because it predicts attrition better. But the evaluation demonstrates that the model identifies students who respond to support in the current intervention context — a different and more limited claim.

Verdict: critique_wins.
